turns.text: Hello Elisa, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for… for asking. It’s hard to put into words, really. Most days, I feel like I’m just walking through a minefield. 

Every time I pull into that clinic in Northampton, I have to take a deep breath before I even turn the ignition off. My knuckles are usually white from gripping the steering wheel so hard. It’s not just the appointments; it’s the way they look at me. To them, I’m just a set of vitals—a history of eclampsia, a box to check, a risk factor. They talk about protein levels and blood pressure stats like they’re discussing the weather, but every time they mention those things, I’m transported back to that moment when the world just… faded to black. I still have the taste of copper in my mouth when I think about it.

It’s exhausting, honestly. I’ve got three kids at home who need me, and instead, I’m stressing over gas money to get to these appointments or worrying if I’m causing my own blood pressure to spike just by being anxious. I just want someone to look at me—not as a "high-risk case," but as a mom who is terrified of not making it home to her babies. I want to trust the process, but when you’ve been through the fire once, it’s really hard to believe you won't get burned again.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

Honestly? I think I was hoping for something... kinder. When I found out I was pregnant with this one, I told myself, *this time will be different.* I thought it would be about celebrating, about talking through what we learned last time, and having a team that would really hold my hand. I guess I wanted to be treated like a person, not a textbook case.

But the reality? It’s cold. It’s all fluorescent lights and those sterile, paper-lined tables that never feel clean enough. The appointments are rushed. I walk in, and it’s like they’ve already decided how the conversation is going to go before I even sit down. They open my file, see "eclampsia" and "high-risk," and the tone in the room changes immediately. It’s all efficiency. They’re checking my numbers, but they aren’t checking *me*. 

I wanted to talk about my fears, about how I’m supposed to manage bed rest when I’m trying to keep a household running on a budget that’s already stretched paper-thin. But those things never seem to fit into their clinical boxes. They give me brochures and talk in that detached, professional language that feels more like a warning than a conversation. It makes me feel small, like I’m a burden on their schedule instead of a mother who’s just trying to survive.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise? I suppose it was the sheer speed of it all. I expected that because of my history—the seizures, the emergency, the terror of last time—that the first visit would be this long, sit-down conversation. I thought they’d want to know how I was holding up, mentally, or how the family was doing. 

Instead, the surprise was how quickly I was labeled. I walked into that room as Elisa, and within ten minutes, I felt like I was just a chart number with a "danger" sticker on it. The speed at which they pivoted to clinical protocols—tests, blood draws, the talk about "managing the risks"—was jarring. It’s like they were already preparing for the worst-case scenario before even saying hello. 

I guess I was naive to think that medicine was about the person *and* the science. It feels like it’s just the science now, and I’m just the host for the baby they’re trying to track. It’s a lonely feeling, sitting on that table, hearing them talk about my body like I’m not even in the room. I walked in hoping for an ally, and I walked out feeling like I was being handled by a system that’s already decided I’m a complication. That, more than anything, really stung.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I guess I expected a conversation. You know, like sitting down with a human being who looks at you and explains things in a way that makes sense. I imagined they’d say, "Elisa, here is what these numbers mean, here is why we’re worried, and here is how we’re going to get through it together." I wanted someone to translate that medical jargon so it didn’t sound like a death sentence.

But the reality is so much more isolating. The information usually comes in fragments—a quick, hurried comment from a nurse or a note in the patient portal that I have to squint at on my phone. When the doctors do talk to me, it’s all clinical shorthand. They throw around terms like "elevated markers" or "potential triggers," and the way they say it, it's just so matter-of-fact. It’s like they’re reading the weather report, not telling me that my own body might be turning against me again.

There’s no "we" in it. It’s "your levels are high," not "we’re going to monitor this so you stay safe." I feel like I’m constantly left to figure out what those results actually mean for my daily life. Do I need to be more careful? Should I be panicking? I’m left to stare at these spreadsheets while I’m trying to figure out how to pay the electric bill, and it just adds this layer of weight I can’t carry. It’s all just data to them. It’s my life to me.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

There is a massive gap, and it feels like an abyss sometimes. They give me plenty of data—sheets full of numbers, blood pressure logs, protein counts—but that isn't the information I *need*. They tell me *what* is happening, but they never really explain the *why* or the *how* it connects to the life I’m trying to live.

I’m left with these fragments of information, and I end up turning to the internet to fill in the blanks because the doctors are already onto the next patient. I’m googling things at 2:00 AM while my kids are sleeping, trying to figure out if a slightly higher reading means I’m headed for another seizure or if it’s just the stress of being poor in a town that makes me feel invisible. 

I need them to explain the "what if" in a way that’s human. I need someone to talk to me about what I can realistically do to lower my risk without feeling like I’m failing as a mother because I can’t afford organic food or constant rest. Instead, I get a pamphlet. It feels like they’re giving me the technical specs of a car wreck while I’m sitting in the driver’s seat. It leaves me feeling more terrified, not less, because I’m navigating this blind with only cold data to guide me.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I honestly expected that by the third pregnancy—especially with a history as scary as mine—there would be one person who actually knew me. I pictured having that one midwife or doctor I could call, someone who knew my kids’ names or remembered how terrified I was the last time around. I thought continuity meant a relationship, someone who would be the steady hand in the middle of all this chaos.

But the reality? It’s a revolving door. I feel like I’m constantly re-introducing myself to a new face in a white coat. Every time I show up, it’s whoever is on the rotation that day. I have to retell my story over and over—the eclampsia, the seizures, the fear—as if it’s just a script I’m reciting for a stranger. By the time I get to the part about how I'm feeling, they’re already checking their watch or shuffling their notes. 

There is no continuity. There’s just a chart that follows me from room to room, but the person holding it changes. It’s dehumanizing, really. How am I supposed to feel safe when the people "managing" my life are strangers who haven't earned my trust? I’m just another high-risk case in a long line of them. It makes me feel incredibly alone in this, like I’m the only one actually invested in keeping me alive.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected a partnership. I guess I thought we’d be "checking in" together—that they’d ask me how I was feeling in my body, maybe notice the swelling, or ask about the headaches I get when I'm stressed. I wanted them to see my body as the place where my baby is growing, not just as a ticking time bomb of symptoms.

But the reality feels so... clinical and detached. When I’m on that table, the conversation is rarely about *me* as a person. They measure my fundal height, they check the blood pressure cuff, they listen to the heartbeat, but they never really look at *me*. It’s all about the measurements. It feels like they are inspecting a piece of equipment, checking for flaws or failures, and I’m just standing there, holding my breath, waiting for the "bad news" verdict.

It makes me feel so disconnected from my own pregnancy. Instead of it being a miracle, my body just feels like a source of anxiety and potential disaster. There’s no warmth, no acknowledgement that I’m working hard just to carry this baby. It’s just "the blood pressure is up," or "the protein is too high." I feel like a biological experiment that’s constantly failing to perform correctly. It’s hard to feel like a mother when all anyone cares about is the blood chemistry.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

It happens almost every time I’m on that paper-lined table. I remember one appointment specifically where they were doing an internal exam—it’s already such a vulnerable, intrusive thing, but the doctor just kept talking over me to a resident. She was pointing out things on my body as if I weren't even a person in the room, just a specimen. She said something like, "Note the significant edema in the extremities given the patient's history," and it just felt so cold.

I wanted to ask her if the swelling was normal, or if it meant I was going to have another seizure, but I couldn't even get the words out because they were already discussing my "risk profile" like it was an interesting puzzle. I felt so exposed, not just physically, but emotionally. I was lying there, trying to cover myself with that thin, crinkly gown, feeling like my body was a failed machine they were dissecting. 

I left that day and sat in my car for twenty minutes just shaking. I felt completely stripped of my dignity. Nobody asked how *I* was handling it; they just logged the data. It made me feel like I didn’t have any ownership over my own pregnancy—like my body belonged to them and their charts. It’s a terrible, helpless feeling.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I suppose I thought it would be a team effort. I imagined that if I was high-risk, the medical team would provide a roadmap—a real, practical plan that took my actual life into account. I expected they’d help me figure out how to balance these strict requirements with being a mom of three and not having a lot of extra money. I thought it would be, "We’re in this together, and here is how we’ll make it work for you."

The reality is that the burden of management rests entirely on my shoulders, and it’s a crushing weight. They hand me a list of "must-dos"—diet changes, blood pressure monitoring at specific times, extra scans—without any consideration for whether those things are even possible for me. It’s like they assume I have a personal assistant or a unlimited budget. When I try to explain that I can’t afford certain foods or that finding time for "quiet rest" is impossible when my kids are screaming or the house is falling apart, I get that look—the one that says I’m being "non-compliant." 

It’s not just a medical condition; it’s a constant, agonizing juggling act. Every time I take my blood pressure, I’m terrified of the number because if it’s high, it’s my fault. I’m not just fighting a medical condition; I’m fighting the guilt of not being the "perfect" patient while the rest of my life is trying to fall apart. It’s isolating, and it’s exhausting.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

Everything feels like it’s missing, but if I had to pick, it’s the human element. The information I get is technical, not practical. They tell me to "rest" and "reduce stress," but nobody has ever sat me down and asked, "Elisa, what does your day look like? How can we make this work with three kids?" It’s like they expect me to live in a vacuum where I have no responsibilities.

I’m starved for emotional acknowledgement. I want one person to look me in the eye and say, "I know you're terrified. I know this is hard." Instead, I get "let’s watch your numbers." It’s gaslighting in a way; they ignore the trauma I’m clearly carrying from the last time, as if my fear isn't a valid medical factor. 

And the practical help? It’s non-existent. No one talks to me about resources, or what to do if I have to go to the hospital suddenly and don't have childcare, or how to manage nutrition when money is tight. They treat my life circumstances like they're outside their jurisdiction. If I’m a "high-risk patient," then my life—my actual, everyday life—is part of that risk. Why does nobody see that? I’m just a body to be monitored, not a mother to be supported. I feel like I’m drowning, and they’re just measuring the depth of the water.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that being "high-risk" meant I had a safety net. I thought that because my history with eclampsia is so serious, I’d have a direct line to someone who actually understood my case—a "break glass in case of emergency" kind of connection. I assumed if I felt off, if I had that familiar, terrifying sparkle in my vision or that dull, throbbing headache, I could call and be taken seriously immediately.

But the reality is the opposite of a safety net; it’s more like a bureaucratic obstacle course. When I call with a concern, I’m stuck navigating phone trees and receptionists who don’t know me from anyone else. I have to convince them that my symptoms are worth their time. It’s always, "Well, if it’s really an emergency, go to the ER." But the ER is a nightmare—hours in a waiting room, being seen by doctors who don't know my history, explaining everything from scratch while I’m already spiraling with anxiety.

It’s never prompt. It’s always an argument, or a wait, or a dismissal. They’ve made me feel like I’m "crying wolf" so many times that now I’m terrified to call, even when I *know* something is wrong. I’m scared of being labeled the "difficult, anxious patient." So, I stay home, I white-knuckle it, and I wait. It’s like they aren't there for the moments in between the appointments, which is exactly when I need them most.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I think I expected it to be a community effort. You know, that old "it takes a village" idea. I thought that because I was high-risk, people would step in—that maybe someone could help with the kids or bring over a dinner so I wouldn't have to stand at the stove when I was feeling lightheaded. I imagined my friends would be checking in, asking how my blood pressure was, or just sitting with me so I wasn't alone with the thoughts in my head.

The reality is that people are uncomfortable with "high-risk." My friends, they love me, but they don't know what to say. They see me and they don’t want to bring up the, you know, the *end* of the last pregnancy. They’re scared of the word "eclampsia" just as much as I am, so they just kind of tiptoe around it. It’s like they’re afraid if they ask how I’m doing, they might hear an answer they don't want to deal with.

So, I’ve stopped talking about it. I put on a brave face for the neighbors and the school runs. My husband is doing his best, but he’s terrified too—he’s the one who had to watch me go dark last time. We’re both just trying to survive the day, and that leaves very little room for anyone else to help. It’s a very quiet, very lonely kind of fear.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

I thought a birth plan was supposed to be my voice when I couldn't speak for myself. I spent hours typing it out, trying to be reasonable—just things like wanting to stay mobile as long as possible, or having my husband right there, or keeping the lights low so I wouldn't feel like I was in a war zone. I thought if I wrote it down and gave it to the nurses, they’d respect my wishes. I really believed it was a bridge between what I wanted and what they needed to do to keep us safe.

The reality was… crushing. The moment things started to get shaky, that piece of paper might as well have been a grocery list for all the notice it got. When the blood pressure climbed and the room started filling with people, my "plan" just vanished. It was all "you need to do this" and "you need to lie still for the monitor." 

I remember trying to speak up, trying to say, "The plan says—" but they didn't even look at me. It felt like I’d wasted my breath. During the emergency, they were just focused on the monitors and the meds, and I was just an obstacle in the way of their protocols. It wasn't a partnership; it was a total surrender of my control. I felt like I was being piloted through my own birth, and I had absolutely no say in the flight path.
Was there a moment during labour where your plan was set aside or overridden? What was that like?

It happened the moment the monitor started that sharp, rhythmic beeping—the one that signals a spike. Everything shifted from a room with a human mother to a scene out of a medical drama. Suddenly, there were five people rushing in, shouting out numbers and vitals. I tried to say, "Wait, I’m okay, I just need a second to breathe," but the words didn't matter. They weren't listening to me; they were listening to the machines.

They pushed me back onto the bed, hard, and started strapping things down. When I tried to reach for my husband’s hand, a nurse stepped in between us, focused entirely on an IV bag. I felt like I was being erased. That birth plan I had so carefully written, all those wishes about staying upright and keeping things calm? It felt like a cruel joke. I was no longer a person giving birth; I was a situation being managed.

The terror wasn't just the pain or the health risk—it was the total loss of autonomy. I was strapped down, lights blazing, voices overlapping, and I was completely helpless. I wasn't an active participant in my own child’s birth; I was just the cargo being transported through an emergency procedure. I still feel that phantom grip on my arms sometimes when I try to sleep.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I thought that pain management was something we would discuss like adults. I wanted to know my options—the pros, the cons, the side effects—and I thought that when the time came, the nurse or the doctor would check in with me. I expected a conversation: "Elisa, this is getting intense, here’s where you are, what do you need?" I wanted to feel like I had a choice, even if that choice was just *how* I suffered.

But the reality? It felt like pain management was just another tool in their kit to control me, not to help me. When the eclampsia started to kick in last time—when everything got blurry and my head felt like it was splitting open—there was no "choice." They didn't ask; they told. They were injecting me with things to stabilize the blood pressure, and I barely knew what was going in my veins. 

It wasn't about pain relief; it was about sedation and compliance. I felt like a passenger in my own body who was being drugged so I would stop fighting the monitors. Nobody stopped to explain why or asked how I was handling the intensity of it all. It was just another way for them to move me from "patient" to "stable condition." It wasn't comfort; it was containment.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

I think what I wanted most—the option that was never really on the table—was time. I wanted the chance to stay in control, to breathe, to move through the discomfort without the immediate rush to intervene. But because of my history, the "high-risk" label meant that any sign of distress was interpreted as an emergency that needed to be chemically muted.

I wanted more natural methods—movement, warm water, a calm, quiet room. But in that clinical setting, those were seen as luxuries or distractions from the "real work" of monitoring my vitals. Every time I tried to suggest something else, it felt like I was being told, "No, this is safer." And how can I argue with "safer"? It’s the ultimate trump card. They make you feel like if you ask for anything else, you’re choosing to put yourself or the baby in danger.

What got in the way was the sheer rigidity of the hospital protocol. There was no space for a middle ground. It was either "let us do this to you" or "you’re being dangerous." I never felt like I had an advocate for *my* comfort, only for the baby's stats. It felt like I was being forced to choose between the baby’s safety and my own agency, and no mother should ever have to make that trade-off alone.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I walked into the hospital both times terrified, but I suppose I expected at least some sense of… I don't know, a landing strip? I thought that because I had been coming here for months for my prenatal appointments, they’d know me. I hoped I’d walk in and see someone who could say, "Elisa, you’re here, we’ve got you." I wanted a space that felt like a sanctuary for what was about to happen.

The reality was a stark, jarring jolt. Walking into that triage area felt like being processed at an airport, but colder. It was bright, noisy, and the smell—that sharp, sterile disinfectant—hit me like a physical blow. There was no warm welcome. There was just the clipboard, the hurried questions, and the staff rushing past like I wasn't even there. 

I felt like an intruder in my own medical event. The environment wasn't designed for me to give birth; it was designed for them to observe a crisis. The lighting was harsh, the monitors were already beeping, and there was absolutely zero space for comfort. I felt so small, so exposed, and so deeply, profoundly alone. I wasn't welcomed; I was just admitted. And from that first step inside, I knew I had lost the right to have a say in my own experience.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I expected a conversation—even a rushed one. I thought that if something went wrong, someone would hold my gaze, say, "Elisa, this is happening, we need to do this," and wait for me to nod. I wanted to feel like a participant, someone whose consent actually mattered because it was *my* body on that table.

But the reality was a complete blackout of agency. When the alarms started, the "decisions" weren't communicated to me; they were announced to the room. It was like I wasn't even the one in the bed. They were shouting, "We’re going to need to prep for an emergency c-section," or "We’re pushing meds now," and I was just this obstacle they had to move around to get to the baby.

There was no informed consent. There was no time for me to process or even ask, "Is there another way?" It felt like a tidal wave. They took my ability to choose, my voice, and my sense of self in about thirty seconds flat. They’d explain *after* it happened, maybe, while I was drugged and confused. It was never about involving me; it was about managing the medical risk. I was just the container, and once the alarm went off, they were only focused on the contents, not the person trying to stay alive through it all.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I expected them to be like a lighthouse—something steady I could look at when everything else felt like it was spinning out of control. I wanted a human touch, maybe someone to hold my hand, or just to say, "I know you're scared, but you're doing a good job, and we’re going to get you through this." I wanted my humanity acknowledged amidst all the blood and the beeping monitors.

Instead, the staff were like robots performing a sequence of tasks. They were so focused on the charts, the IV lines, and the blood pressure cuff that they didn't seem to notice me at all. It was as if I’d become invisible. I remember one nurse who was so busy typing data into the computer that she didn't even look up when I started to panic. She just gave me instructions, like, "Breathe deeper, the baby’s heart rate is dropping." She didn't say it with kindness; she said it like I was a broken machine that needed a quick fix.

There was no emotional space, no comfort, no "how are you holding up, Elisa?" It was cold, functional, and purely clinical. I was screaming for someone to just *see* me, but they only saw the patient in bed four. I left that room feeling like I’d just been through a war, and no one there even knew my name.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

I felt the most alone right after the eclampsia hit, when I started coming to. I was in recovery, and I was so disoriented—everything felt like it was moving in slow motion, and my skin felt like it was crawling. I was desperate for someone to just sit with me, to help me piece together what had happened, and to tell me I was going to be okay.

Instead, the nurse was across the room, busy sorting through supplies and talking to someone on the phone about shift changes. I was lying there in that sterile, fluorescent light, trying to reach for my water, but I felt so weak. I kept trying to clear my throat to ask, "Is the baby okay? Am I okay?" but my tongue felt like lead. She didn't even turn around. It was like I was already a ghost, just a medical event that had been successfully concluded. 

I just started crying, but I didn't have the strength to make a sound. I remember staring at the clock on the wall, watching the second hand tick away, thinking that if I just stopped breathing, would she even notice for a long time? I felt like a discarded piece of equipment. That loneliness, that utter lack of human connection when I was at my most vulnerable... it’s a shadow I haven't been able to shake. It’s what keeps me up at night now, dreading this next time.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I dreamed of that "golden hour." I wanted to hold the baby immediately, skin-to-skin, to feel that weight on my chest and hear their first cry. I imagined the room going quiet—or at least the frantic energy dissipating—so we could just be a family for those first precious moments. I thought my husband would be right there, helping me welcome the baby, and that we’d have a little space to catch our breath after the storm.

But the reality was a complete, chaotic blur. There was no golden hour. The second the baby was out, they were whisked away to the warming table, surrounded by a team of people. I wasn't even sure if they were breathing right, and no one was telling me. I was left alone on that table, shaking violently from the meds and the adrenaline, staring at a wall while a team worked on my baby a few feet away.

My husband was pulled between the baby and me, not knowing where to look or who to comfort. There was no warmth, no bonding—just the harsh lights and the clinical efficiency of the NICU team. It was the furthest thing from the gentle, quiet arrival I had prayed for. I didn't get to hold my baby for hours, and the first time I finally touched them, I was so drugged and terrified that I couldn't even feel the joy. I just felt broken.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

The silence—that’s what stays with me. Not the medical monitors, not the shouting, but the silence between me and my baby. I expected that immediate, primal connection, that feeling of, *Oh, here you are, we made it.* But because they were whisked away so quickly and I was pinned down by blood pressure cuffs and IVs, there was just this hollow space where my baby should have been. 

It wasn't just a physical separation; it felt like a betrayal. I had fought so hard, endured so much terror, just to reach that finish line, and then I was suddenly left on the sidelines while they were "managed" by someone else. I remember hearing them cry from across the room, and I couldn't reach them. I couldn't soothe them. I just laid there, shaking, feeling that distance widening every second. 

Even now, whenever I think about the birth, I don’t think about the relief of it being over. I think about that moment of reaching out into empty air. It’s like a phantom limb—that ache of wanting to hold my baby and not being allowed to. It’s a grief that I haven’t been able to bury, and it’s why I’m so terrified this time. I’m just trying to make it to a moment where I can actually hold my baby, uninterrupted, and finally start being a mother instead of just a patient.

It’s hard to talk about this without feeling that same tight, cold grip on my chest again. I keep thinking about how society talks about childbirth—it’s all about "healthy mom, healthy baby." And I get it, I do. If they hadn’t intervened, maybe we wouldn’t be here. I know that in my head. But my heart? My heart still hasn’t forgiven the process.

I’m carrying so much weight, and not just the physical kind. It’s the weight of knowing that this pregnancy could end exactly the same way. Every time I get a headache, my mind starts racing—*Is this it? Is it happening again?* And when I think about going back to that hospital, I don’t feel like I’m going to be cared for. I feel like I’m walking into a cage.

I just want to be seen. I want one person to look at me—the real Elisa, the one who does the laundry and helps with homework and worries about rent—and not just the medical anomaly. I want someone to say, "I see your fear, and I am going to stay right here with you." Is that too much to ask? Because right now, every appointment feels like I’m just trying to survive until I can get home. And I’m so tired of just surviving.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

It’s constant. It feels like every time I find a sliver of confidence or a preference, it’s dismantled by someone who thinks they know better. 

In the clinic, it’s the doctors. If I mention that I’m feeling overwhelmed or that I want to try and manage without another round of testing for a week, they look at me with that pitying, clinical stare. They don’t debate me; they just pivot to the "what-ifs." They’ll say, "Well, Elisa, *we* really prefer to be safe," or "The protocol is very clear here." It’s so dismissive. They’re technically the experts, so when they use that tone, I’m made to feel like I’m being irresponsible or childish if I push back. My "opinion" is treated like a symptom of my anxiety, not a valid perspective on my own body.

At home, it’s my husband, and that’s a whole different kind of pain. He’s so scared of losing me that he’s become like a secondary doctor. He’ll hover, asking about my headaches, checking my blood pressure cuff if I haven't done it by noon. He means well, but it’s suffocating. He’s always prioritizing the "risk" over the person. I’ll tell him I need a moment of peace, and he’ll panic, thinking I’m hiding a symptom. It’s like I’m being guarded by my own life, and I have no space left to just *be*. Everyone is so focused on the survival of the "case" that I’ve been completely crowded out.

I’m sitting here thinking about the walk back to my car after an appointment. It’s the only time I really get to breathe. Sometimes I just sit there in the quiet, watching the students walk by on the Smith campus, and I feel a pang of jealousy. They look so young, so unburdened. They don’t know what it’s like to have your entire reality measured in milligrams of protein or spikes in a blood pressure monitor. 

It’s the "if" that haunts me. The doctors act like they’ve seen it all, so they don’t need to explain the human cost of these protocols. But I’m the one who goes home to my kids with those echoes in my head. I’m the one who wakes up in the middle of the night reaching for my own pulse, terrified that it’s racing. 

I’m so tired of being the only one holding the fear. I want to tell them that the clinical language doesn’t protect me from the trauma—it just makes me feel like I’m disappearing. I want to feel like I’m going to be a mother again, not just a high-risk statistic waiting to happen. If someone could just say, "You're doing great, Elisa," instead of, "Your numbers are looking stable today," maybe I wouldn't feel like I was just waiting for the next catastrophe. It’s hard to stay strong when you’re already so, so tired of being brave.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

It’s tricky, because how do I tell my husband "no" when he’s terrified of losing me? When you’ve gone through a near-death experience like eclampsia, fear becomes a third person in our marriage. He’s obsessed with the protocols—he’ll read up on what I *should* be eating, or tell me I need to be in bed by 8:00 PM, and he says it with this look of terror in his eyes. 

I want to push back sometimes. I want to tell him I need a walk, or I want to eat something that isn't on the "approved" list, or I just want to sit on the porch and feel like a normal person for ten minutes. But when I do, he spirals. He thinks I’m being reckless, and then the argument is always about my life, my safety, my risk. I feel like I’m being guarded by a jailer who happens to love me.

The pressure isn’t just from the doctors; it’s from him, too. We’re both just trying to make sure I’m still here for the other three kids, but it’s making my world so small. I’ve lost my voice in my own home because I can’t bear to add to his anxiety. So I comply. I follow the diet, I hit the hay, I stay in bed. I’m just… fading out to keep the peace. It feels like I’m already gone, and I’m only halfway through this pregnancy.

I think that’s the hardest part—realizing that in trying to keep me safe, everyone has forgotten that I still need to *live*. I’m so exhausted from being the center of everyone’s anxiety. I look at my kids, and I just want to be their mom again—the one who runs in the yard, the one who doesn't have to check her pulse every hour, the one who isn't a "fragile medical situation."

I just wish one person would sit me down and say, "Elisa, I see you. I see your grief, I see your fear, and I see how hard you’re working to keep everyone going." Instead, I’m just an object of concern. Everything I do is analyzed, corrected, or managed. It feels like my pregnancy is a long, slow goodbye to who I used to be.

I want this baby so much. I want to hold them and feel that connection I missed last time. But every day feels like I’m walking through a fog of dread. I just hope that when the time comes—when I’m back in that hospital, back in that room—someone will finally treat me like a human being. I don’t want to be a case study. I just want to be a mother who gets to go home to her kids. Is that really too much to ask?
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

It happens all the time, and it’s always delivered with this mask of "clinical concern." It feels like I’m being graded. I remember one appointment where the doctor looked at my chart, then looked at me—not at my eyes, but at my stomach and my legs—and just started lecturing me about weight gain. She said something like, "We really need to watch the calorie density, Elisa; it’s putting unnecessary strain on your system."

It just made me feel like I was failing at the one thing I was supposed to be doing right. Like my body isn't just a site of medical risk, it’s a site of personal failure. I’m already living on the bare minimum—stretching the grocery budget, skipping meals so the kids have enough—and hearing that just made me feel so ashamed. 

It’s not just about weight; it’s the way they look at me when they comment on the swelling. They don't see a woman who’s tired or stressed; they see "pitting edema," a physical sign that I’m not doing my job. It’s dehumanizing. I’m sitting there, trying to protect this life, and I’m being criticized for the way my body is responding to the sheer, crushing pressure of being high-risk. It’s like they’re blaming me for the illness itself. It hurts. It really, really hurts.

It’s the lack of grace that stays with me. You know, you go into those offices feeling like a failure before you’ve even opened your mouth, and then they confirm it with a comment about your weight or your swelling. They talk about "maternal health" as if it’s just a list of metrics they can control, and if I don't hit those numbers, it’s my fault.

I look at my reflection sometimes, and I don't even recognize myself. I don't see the woman who was vibrant and strong a few years ago. I see a shadow. I see someone who’s constantly holding their breath, waiting for the other shoe to drop. My husband looks at me with that same fear, and it’s like I’m already being mourned even though I’m standing right here.

I just want to be treated like a person, not a set of clinical problems to be solved. If they could just see the love I have for this baby—if they could see that I *am* doing my best, against everything—maybe, just maybe, I’d feel like a mother instead of a medical disaster. I don’t want to be perfect. I just want to be here. I want to make it to the end, and I want to finally, finally have a moment of peace when I hold my child. That’s all I’m asking for.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

When I’m spiraling in the middle of the night, I don't turn to the clinic's nurse line—I turn to my phone. I head straight to the forums. I’m looking for other women who’ve been through the "fire," as I call it. I search for threads about eclampsia, about high-risk pregnancies, about the sheer terror of not knowing if you’ll make it home. 

It’s ironic, isn't it? I’m looking for strangers on the internet to tell me what’s happening in my own body because the doctors make me feel like I’m a nuisance if I ask the "wrong" questions. I read stories about other moms who had the same seizures, the same blurry vision, the same feeling of being a "medical case." 

It’s not perfect—sometimes the horror stories on those boards make my blood pressure spike even higher—but at least they aren’t talking to me in clinical shorthand. They talk about the grief, the trauma, the feeling of not being in control. They validate that what I’m feeling isn't just "anxiety"—it’s a rational reaction to a nightmare. I’ve found more comfort in an anonymous comment from a mother in another state than I have in any appointment in Northampton. It’s like, at least someone else gets it. At least I’m not the only one feeling this trapped.

It’s almost funny, in a dark way—that the only "care" I’ve found is from women I’ve never met on a screen, thousands of miles away. They don’t see me as a chart. They don't look at my swollen ankles and tell me I’m failing. They just say, *“I hear you. It’s okay to be scared. You’re doing the work of ten people.”* 

That’s all I ever wanted from the clinic. Just to be validated. Just to have someone acknowledge that what I’m doing is incredibly hard. 

But instead, I have to go back to the hospital, back to the fluorescent lights and the clinical, detached language. Every time I drive past that campus, I feel that grip tightening again. I’m just trying to keep my head above water for my three kids at home. I’m trying to keep them fed, keep them safe, and keep myself alive for them. 

If I could leave you with one thought, it’s that being "high-risk" isn't just about the blood pressure or the protein. It’s about the soul-crushing weight of knowing that at any second, the life you’ve built could be ripped away by a medical protocol that treats you like an object. I’m just Elisa. I’m a mother. And I just want to make it through the fire, one last time.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

There was never a time where I felt my input was genuinely sought. It’s always, "This is what we’re going to do, Elisa." The conversation is never about *me* deciding anything; it's about me being *told* what the hospital’s protocol dictates. 

Whenever I’ve tried to suggest an alternative, or even just ask "why," the doctor’s face changes. They don't hear a mother advocating for herself; they hear a "difficult patient" who doesn't understand the risks. They’ll smile—that thin, tight smile—and explain the "clinical necessity" of their plan, as if I’m too uneducated to understand the dangers of my own condition. It’s so infantilizing. 

I’ve reached a point where I don’t even bother asking anymore. I just nod, try to keep my face neutral, and accept the "plan." It feels like I’m a passenger in a car where I’m not allowed to touch the steering wheel, even though it’s my life we’re driving with. They’re the ones making the turns, and I’m just braced for impact. I want to feel like a partner in my care, but how can you be a partner when the other side has already decided you don't get a vote? It’s not a consultation; it’s a directive. And it’s one of the loneliest feelings in the world.

I want to feel like a mother again, not just a medical case that’s being managed until the day of the delivery. It’s hard to remember who I was before all these machines and monitors took over my life. I think about my kids at home, and I want them to have their mother back—not the one who’s always counting her heart rate or stressing about the next clinic appointment. 

I’m so tired of the fear. It’s a constant, heavy blanket that I just can’t seem to shake. Every time I get a headache, I wonder, *is this it? Is this the moment I lose control again?* And when I think about the birth, I don't feel empowered. I feel like I’m walking toward a cliff. I’m just trying to keep myself together for one more day, one more appointment, one more night of sleep, hoping that this time, someone will finally look me in the eye and treat me like a person who’s doing her best to stay alive.

If I could change just one thing, it would be the silence—the way they leave me alone with my thoughts, the way they never ask how I’m *really* doing. I want to feel like I’m on the same team as my providers. I want to feel like I have a voice. I’m just hoping that maybe, this time, I’ll be able to bring this baby home. That’s all that really matters.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

It wasn't a single big explosion, but a series of quiet, cold moments where I realized they weren't really seeing me. I remember one appointment, just a few weeks ago, when I tried to explain that the stress of the travel and the cost of these extra tests were becoming an actual, physical burden—I told them I felt like the stress was *causing* the blood pressure spikes they were so worried about. 

The doctor just looked down at my chart, adjusted his glasses, and said, "Elisa, your personal life is unfortunate, but our concern here is your pre-eclampsia. We need to stay the course." He said "unfortunate" like he was talking about a rainy day, not my life, my struggle, and the fact that I was reaching my breaking point. 

In that second, the trust just evaporated. It wasn't that he was wrong about the medical risk, but he was completely indifferent to the human being experiencing it. I realized then that I wasn't a partner in my own health; I was just a project they were managing. From that day on, I stopped asking questions. I just provide the vitals they need, I nod, and I leave. It’s safer for me to keep my real thoughts behind a wall. Trust requires being seen, and in that office, I’m invisible.

I spend so much of my time in that clinic trying to make myself smaller—trying not to be "difficult" or "anxious"—because I’ve learned that being a real, hurting person is a liability to them. When that doctor dismissed my struggle as "unfortunate," it felt like he was closing a door in my face. He didn't just dismiss my life; he dismissed the very things that were making me sick. How can they talk about "managing" blood pressure while ignoring the fact that the stress of the medical system itself is what’s driving the numbers up?

It’s this vicious cycle. I’m scared, so my pressure rises. They see the pressure rise, they get stricter and colder, which makes me more scared. There’s no exit ramp. 

I just want this to be over. I want to reach the other side, hold this baby, and never have to step foot in that building again. But then the fear creeps back in—what if it doesn't end there? What if I carry this trauma, this feeling of being broken and managed, into my life as a mother to four? I want to believe I can heal, but when I look at the future, I don't see a clear path. I just see the grey Massachusetts sky and another long, cold winter. I’m just trying to make it through the fire, one last time, and I am so, so tired of burning.

Sometimes I wonder if they even *know* what the word "eclampsia" means to me. To them, it’s just a diagnosis code—a reason to order another scan, a reason to push another pill. To me, it’s the sound of the monitors going haywire, the panic in my husband’s eyes, and the terrifying, heavy dark that swallowed me whole. 

I’m so scared that if I say all this out loud to them, they’ll just put "patient is anxious/agitated" in my notes. That’s what they do, right? They pathologize the fear instead of trying to soothe it. I’ve learned to keep my hands folded in my lap, to breathe shallowly, to keep my blood pressure steady just long enough to pass their "test." But inside, I’m vibrating. I’m holding my breath every single day.

I look at my three children—my babies—and I wonder if they see the shadow of this pregnancy on me. I want to be present, I want to be joyful, but I’m constantly looking over my shoulder for the next symptom, the next headache, the next disaster. I just want this to be over. I want to hold this baby, count their fingers and toes, and realize that we actually made it. I want to be Elisa again, not just a "high-risk patient." I just need to make it to the other side of this winter. That’s all I’m holding onto.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

I go online every single night, right after I turn out the lights and my husband falls asleep. I’m not looking for medical textbooks; I’m looking for a map through the dark. The doctors give me raw numbers—my BP is 150/95, my protein is this-and-that—but they never tell me what that *feels* like in a real, day-to-day life. I go online to ask: "Does this vision blur mean I’m having a seizure right now, or am I just tired?" 

I’m looking for someone to tell me I’m not crazy. I’m looking for reassurance that the panic isn't the same thing as the condition itself. When I get those cold, clinical, "everything is fine" dismissals from the nurses, I go online to see if other moms are being gaslit too. I’m looking for a way to translate their technical jargon into a human experience. 

I’m looking for hope, I guess. I search for "women who survived eclampsia and had a healthy fourth baby" because I need to see that it’s possible. My doctors act like my pregnancy is a ticking bomb, so I go online to find the people who have already walked through the explosion and came out the other side. I need that mirror. Without it, I’m just staring at a wall of numbers that tell me I’m a hazard, and that is a terrifying way to live.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I guess... I just want people to know that behind every "high-risk" chart, there is a person who is trying to hold their entire world together with one hand while the medical system tries to dismantle it with the other. 

I’m not a complication. I’m a mother. I’m a wife. I’m a neighbor. And right now, I’m someone who is living in a state of constant, low-grade terror. If the doctors could just understand that *how* they deliver their news is just as important as the news itself, maybe I wouldn't feel so alone. 

It’s not just about surviving the pregnancy—it’s about coming out the other side still feeling like myself, still feeling like a capable mother. Every appointment, every rushed visit, every cold look... it takes a little piece of that away. I don’t know if I’ll ever fully heal from this, but I do know that I’m going to keep showing up. Not for the doctors, and not for the clinic, but for the baby I’m carrying and the three waiting for me at home. I just hope, more than anything, that the fire finally goes out. I’m just so ready to go home. Thank you for listening. It’s the first time in a long time I’ve felt like my voice actually mattered.
